title: Alex Murphy (rugby league)

2018}}{{Rugby league records |id=9071 |title=Alex Murphy |access-date=10 January 2026}} |retired = yes }} '''Alexander James Murphy''' (born 22 April 1939) is an English former professional [[rugby league]] footballer and [[Coach (sport)|coach]]. Sometimes known as 'Murphy the Mouth' and regarded as one of the greatest halfbacks in the history of the British game,{{cite news | title = RL legend Murphy must rest up after heart scare | work = The Huddersfield Daily Examiner | place =UK | publisher = Trinity Mirror North West & North Wales Limited. | date = 5 August 2005 | url = http://www.examiner.co.uk/sport-news/amateur-rugby-league-news/tm_objectid=15823799&method=full&siteid=50060&headline=rl-legend-murphy-must-rest-up-after-heart-scare-name_page.html | access-date =24 April 2010}}{{Cite news | last = Ray French | author-link = Ray French | title = 'The Mouth' returns | work =BBC Sport | place = UK | publisher = [[BBC]] | date = 5 September 2003 | url = http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/3084426.stm | access-date =31 October 2010}} he represented Great Britain in 27 Tests. His club career was played at three clubs, [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]], [[Leigh Centurions|Leigh]] and [[Warrington Wolves|Warrington]]. Murphy assumed a [[player-coach]] role of the last two clubs and expanded his coaching role toward the end of his playing career to include clubs such as [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]], [[Salford Red Devils|Salford]] and [[Huddersfield Giants|Huddersfield]]. He later returned to both Warrington and Leigh respectively as a football manager.{{cite web|url=http://www.rugbyleagueproject.org/coaches/alex-murphy/summary.html|title=Coach Statistics at rugbyleagueproject.org|publisher=rugbyleagueproject.org|date=31 December 2017|access-date=1 January 2018}} He was the first player to captain three different clubs to victory in the [[Challenge Cup]] Final . ==Background== Born in [[St Helens, Merseyside|St. Helens]], [[Lancashire]] on 22 April 1939, Murphy was brought up in [[Thatto Heath]], and at ten years of age had played in both the junior and senior XIIIs at St Austin's School. ==Playing career== Murphy had received town and county schoolboy honours by the time he signed with his native St. Helens for £80 on his 16th birthday in 1955.{{Cite news | last = Barker | first = Neil | title = Murphy joins thousands for Saints farewell | work = [[Manchester Evening News]] | place = UK | publisher = MEN Media | date = 22 September 2010 | url = http://menmedia.co.uk/manchestereveningnews/sport/rugby_league/s/1328511_murphy_joins_thousands_for_saints_farewell | access-date =31 October 2010| archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20100926183046/http://menmedia.co.uk/manchestereveningnews/sport/rugby_league/s/1328511_murphy_joins_thousands_for_saints_farewell| archive-date= 26 September 2010 | url-status= live}} The signing itself was almost akin to a military operation. St. Helens representatives smuggled Murphy 'under cover' to a nearby house until the clock struck midnight to signal Murphy's 16th birthday, and his eligibility to sign professional terms. He was coached from an early age by [[Jim Sullivan (Welsh rugby league)|Jim Sullivan]]. During his [[national service]] Murphy played [[rugby union]] for the [[Royal Air Force]],{{Cite news | last = Shea | first = Julian | title = Murphy clocks up half century | work = BBC Sport | place = UK | publisher = [[BBC]] | date = 22 April 2005 | url = http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/4461281.stm | access-date =31 October 2010}} frequently playing for an Air Force team the same week as playing rugby league for St. Helens. ===St. Helens=== Murphy began his career at St. Helens playing reserve team rugby (known then as the 'A' team). After several "A" team games, Murphy demanded a place in the first team. This demand was refused and so he promptly demanded a transfer. The dispute was settled and Murphy's first team début was against [[Whitehaven R.L.F.C.|Whitehaven]] at [[Knowsley Road]]. Murphy's career at St. Helens went on to be long and successful. While still a teenager, he was selected to tour [[Australasia]] with the [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain side]] in 1958, becoming the youngest touring player at the time,{{Cite news | title = Rugby league's greatest ever players | work = orange.co.uk | page = UK | publisher = Orange | url = http://www.orange.co.uk/sport/pics/6685_8.htm?linkfrom=%3C!--linkfromvariable--%3E&link=link_back&article=sportrugbyleaguelegends | access-date =31 October 2010}} and helping Great Britain to victory in the famous second Test in which they were down to ten men. He scored 21 tries in 20 appearances on that tour.{{Cite news | last = Ledger | first = John | title = Vote for rugby league's greatest ever British XIII | work = [[Yorkshire Post]] | place = UK | publisher = Johnston Press Digital Publishing | date = 22 June 2007 | url = http://www.yorkshirepost.co.uk/wakefield-trinity/Vote-for-rugby-league39s-greatest.2975045.jp | access-date =31 October 2010}} He won the [[Rugby Football League Championship|Championship]] with St. Helens in [[1958–59 Northern Rugby Football League season|1958–59 season]]. The following year he played in Great Britain's [[Rugby League world Cup|World Cup]]-winning side. With his club he claimed the [[Challenge Cup]] in 1961.{{Cite news | title = Murphy pays tribute to Karalius | work = BBC Sport | place = UK | publisher = [[BBC]] | date = 18 December 2008 | url = http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/7789968.stm | access-date =31 October 2010}} On the 1962 Ashes tour he suffered a shoulder injury which caused him to miss three months of the domestic season and there was some speculation he would never play again. On this tour he scored 9 tries in 11 appearances. Murphy became the first British rugby league footballer to have two [[testimonial match]]es. They were at [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]] in 1965, and at [[Warrington Wolves|Warrington]] in 1976. Alex Murphy played at {{rlp|ce}} and scored a [[Try (rugby)|conversion]] in [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]]' 35–12 victory over [[Halifax R.L.F.C.|Halifax]] in the [[Rugby Football League Championship|Championship]] Final during the [[1965–66 Northern Rugby Football League season|1965–66 season]] at [[Station Road, Swinton]] on Saturday 28 May 1966, in front of a crowd of 30,165. Alex Murphy played {{rlp|sh}}, and scored a [[Try (rugby)|try]] in [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]]' 12–6 victory over [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]] in the [[1961 Challenge Cup]] Final during the [[1960–61 Northern Rugby Football League season#Challenge Cup|1960–61 season]] at [[Wembley Stadium (1923)|Wembley Stadium]], [[London]] on Saturday 13 May 1961, in front of a crowd of 94,672, and played at {{rlp|ce}} and scored a [[Try (rugby)|conversion]] in the 21–2 victory over [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]] in the [[1966 Challenge Cup]] Final during the [[1965–66 Northern Rugby Football League season#Challenge Cup|1965–66 season]] at [[Wembley Stadium (1923)|Wembley Stadium]], [[London]] on Saturday 21 May 1966, in front of a crowd of 98,536. Alex Murphy played {{rlp|sh}} in [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]]' 2–12 defeat by [[Oldham R.L.F.C.|Oldham]] in the [[1958 Lancashire Cup]] Final during the [[1958–59 Northern Rugby Football League season|1958–59 season]] at [[Station Road, Swinton]] on Saturday 25 October 1958, played {{rlp|sh}} in the 4–5 defeat by [[Warrington Wolves|Warrington]] in the [[1959 Lancashire Cup]] Final during the [[1959–60 Northern Rugby Football League season|1959–60 season]] at [[Central Park (Wigan)|Central Park]], [[Wigan]] on Saturday 31 October 1959, played {{rlp|sh}} in the 15–9 victory over [[Swinton Lions|Swinton]] in the [[1960 Lancashire Cup]] Final during the [[1960–61 Northern Rugby Football League season|1960–61 season]] at [[Central Park (Wigan)|Central Park]], [[Wigan]] on Saturday 29 October 1960, played {{rlp|sh}}, and scored a [[Try (rugby)|try]] in the 25–9 victory over [[Swinton Lions|Swinton]] in the [[1961 Lancashire Cup]] Final during the [[1961–62 Northern Rugby Football League season|1961–62 season]] at [[Central Park (Wigan)|Central Park]], [[Wigan]] on Saturday 11 November 1961, played {{rlp|sh}} in the 15–4 victory over [[Leigh Centurions|Leigh]] in the [[1962 Lancashire Cup]] Final during the [[1962–63 Northern Rugby Football League season|1962–63 season]] at [[Station Road, Swinton]] on Saturday 26 October 1963, and played {{rlp|sh}} in the 12–4 victory over [[Swinton Lions|Swinton]] in the [[1964 Lancashire Cup]] Final during the [[1964–65 Northern Rugby Football League season|1964–65 season]] at [[Central Park (Wigan)|Central Park]], [[Wigan]] on Saturday 24 October 1964. Alex Murphy played {{rlp|so}} in [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]]' 0–4 defeat by [[Castleford Tigers|Castleford]] in the [[1965 BBC2 Floodlit Trophy]] Final during the [[1965–66 Northern Rugby Football League season|1965–66 season]] at [[Knowsley Road]], [[St Helens, Merseyside|St. Helens]] on Tuesday 14 December 1965 ===Leigh=== Murphy left St. Helens to become [[player-coach]] at Leigh{{When|date=February 2011}} because he was unhappy at being moved to the {{rlp|ce}}s to accommodate the signing of [[Tommy Bishop]]. As the 1966–67 season began, Murphy declined to play for St. Helens. The Australian club, [[North Sydney Bears|North Sydney]] indicated their interest in signing him. At the end of September, Murphy submitted a written transfer request to the St. Helens board who accepted it, putting him on the list at £12,000. North Sydney tabled a bid of £8,000 for Murphy which was accepted. However, at the last minute, Murphy agreed to a 5-year deal with Leigh to become the highest paid coach in the [[Rugby Football League]]. Murphy's first game in charge of his new team was against his former club in a league match at [[Hilton Park (stadium)|Hilton Park]]. Murphy's Leigh overcame a depleted St. Helens side by 29–5. Murphy later recounted in an issue of the [[Rugby Leaguer & League Express|Rugby Leaguer]] some 20 years later that: ''"It never entered my mind to leave Saints in the first place. But events took over and there was a lot of pride involved on both sides and the situation reached the stage where a parting of the ways became inevitable."'' Alex Murphy played {{rlp|sh}}, and was the [[Coach (sport)|coach]], and was [[Man of the match|man-of-the-match]] winning the [[Lance Todd Trophy]] in [[Leigh Centurions|Leigh]]'s 24–7 victory over [[Leeds Rhinos|Leeds]] in the [[1971 Challenge Cup]] Final during the [[1970–71 Northern Rugby Football League season#Challenge Cup|1970–71 season]] at [[Wembley Stadium (1923)|Wembley Stadium]], [[London]] on Saturday 15 May 1971, in front of a crowd of 85,514. This was a match where Murphy's ability to attract controversy was once again exemplified. He was involved in an altercation with Leeds' [[Syd Hynes]] which resulted in Hynes being sent off for headbutting Murphy. Murphy was carried off the pitch on a stretcher, but later returned to the bench and was able to lift the trophy at the end of the game. Over the years, tales of Murphy winking to his teammates as he was carried from the field have endured, and Hynes always maintained his innocence. Alex Murphy played {{rlp|sh}}, and scored a [[Try (rugby)|conversion]] in [[Leigh Centurions|Leigh]]'s 2–11 defeat by [[Swinton Lions|Swinton]] in the [[1969 Lancashire Cup]] Final during the [[1969–70 Northern Rugby Football League season|1969–70 season]] at [[Central Park (Wigan)|Central Park]], [[Wigan]] on Saturday 1 November 1969, and played {{rlp|sh}} in the 7–4 victory over [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]] in the [[1970 Lancashire Cup]] Final [[Replay (sports)|replay]] during the [[1970–71 Northern Rugby Football League season|1970–71 season]] at [[Station Road, Swinton]] on Saturday 28 November 1970. Alex Murphy played {{rlp|sh}}, and was the [[Coach (sport)|coach]] in [[Leigh Centurions|Leigh]]'s 5–8 defeat by [[Castleford Tigers|Castleford]] in the [[1967 BBC2 Floodlit Trophy]] Final during the [[1967–68 Northern Rugby Football League season|1967–68 season]] at [[Headingley Rugby Stadium|Headingley]], [[Leeds]] on Saturday 16 January 1968, and played {{rlp|sh}}, was the [[Coach (sport)|coach]], and scored a [[Try (rugby)|conversion]] in the 11–6 victory over [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]] in the [[1969 BBC2 Floodlit Trophy]] Final during the [[1969–70 Northern Rugby Football League season|1969–70 season]] at [[Central Park (Wigan)|Central Park]], [[Wigan]] on Tuesday 16 December 1969. ===Warrington=== Murphy left Leigh shortly afterwards to become [[player-coach]] at Warrington. Murphy won one Great Britain cap while at Warrington. In 1973 a 20-match unbeaten run in the league helped Warrington win the League Leader's Trophy. The [[1973–74 Northern Rugby Football League season|1973–74 season]] was the most successful at Warrington, with the club winning the [[Challenge Cup]], Captain Morgan trophy, John Player trophy and Club Merit trophy. The highlight was when Murphy [[coach (sport)|coach]]ed, [[Captain (sports)|captain]]ed and played {{rlp|so}} in [[Warrington Wolves|Warrington]]'s 24–9 victory over [[Featherstone Rovers]] in the [[1974 Challenge Cup]] Final during the [[1973–74 Northern Rugby Football League season#Challenge Cup|1973–74 season]] at [[Wembley Stadium (1923)|Wembley Stadium]], [[London]] on Saturday 11 May 1974, in front of a crowd of 77,400.{{Cite news | last = Skentelbery | first = Gary | title = Murphy signs for Wolves! | work = [[Warrington Worldwide]] | place = UK | publisher = Warrington News | date = 23 February 2010 | url = http://www.warrington-worldwide.co.uk/articles/7403/1/Murphy-signs-for-Wolves/Page1.html | access-date =31 October 2010}} He was again the [[coach (sport)|coach]] in the 7–14 defeat by [[Widnes Vikings|Widnes]] in the [[1975 Challenge Cup]] Final during the [[1974–75 Northern Rugby Football League season#Challenge Cup|1974–75 season]] at [[Wembley Stadium (1923)|Wembley Stadium]], [[London]] on Saturday 10 May 1975, in front of a crowd of 85,998, but missed playing in the match through injury, retiring as a player shortly after but remaining as coach of the club until 1978. During his time as the Warrington coach, his old rivals, Widnes succeeded in reaching another Challenge Cup final in 1976, their opponents being St. Helens. Murphy caused controversy by declaring that he would jump off the Runcorn-Widnes Bridge [[Silver Jubilee Bridge]] and into the River Mersey if Widnes won; luckily for him, Widnes were defeated 20 points to 5 and Murphy managed to keep his feet on dry land. Alex Murphy was the [[Coach (sport)|coach]] in [[Warrington Wolves|Warrington]]'s 0–0 draw with [[Salford Red Devils|Salford]] in the [[1974 BBC2 Floodlit Trophy]] Final during the [[1974–75 Northern Rugby Football League season|1974–75 season]] at [[The Willows, Salford]] on Tuesday 17 December 1974, and in the 5–10 defeat by [[Salford Red Devils|Salford]] in the [[1974 BBC2 Floodlit Trophy]] Final [[Replay (sports)|replay]] during the [[1974–75 Northern Rugby Football League season|1974–75 season]] at [[Wilderspool Stadium]], [[Warrington]] on Tuesday 28 January 1975. Alex Murphy was the [[Coach (sport)|coach]] in [[Warrington Wolves|Warrington]]'s 4–0 victory over [[Featherstone Rovers]] in the 1973–74 [[Captain Morgan Trophy]] Final during the [[1973–74 Northern Rugby Football League season|1973–74 season]] at [[The Willows, Salford|The Willows]], [[City of Salford|Salford]] on Saturday 26 January 1974, in front of a crowd of 5,259. ==Coaching career== Upon retirement, Murphy built upon the experience he had acquired as a [[player-coach]] by taking up the reins as a full-time coach. He was co-coach of [[england national rugby league team|England]] with [[Bill Oxley]] during the [[1975 Rugby League World Cup|1975 World Series]]. After Warrington, Murphy was appointed to high-profile roles at [[Salford Red Devils|Salford]] (May 1978 to November 1980), Leigh (1980 to 1982) where in 1982 he guided Leigh to the Division 1 championship and Lancashire cup winners, before taking the role of coach of Wigan in 1982. There, he led them to victory in the John Player Trophy in 1983, and took them to the Challenge Cup Final in 1984, where they lost to Widnes. He left at the start of the next season, following a row with vice chairman [[Maurice Lindsay (rugby league)|Maurice Lindsay]]. In 1988 Murphy was an inaugural inductee into the [[Rugby League Hall of Fame]].{{Cite news | title = Alex Murphy OBE | work = therfl.co.uk | place = UK | publisher = The [[Rugby Football League]] Limited | url = http://www.therfl.co.uk/history_of_the_sport/rugby_league_hall_of_fame/alex_murphy_obe | access-date = 31 October 2010 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20101111055503/http://www.therfl.co.uk/history_of_the_sport/rugby_league_hall_of_fame/alex_murphy_obe | archive-date = 11 November 2010 | url-status = dead }} He took over as coach of St. Helens in 1986, taking them to [[Wembley Stadium (1923)|Wembley Stadium]], [[London]] in 1987, against Halifax, and again in 1989 where they played Wigan. St Helens lost by one point to Halifax, and were humiliated by Wigan in an error-ridden performance two years later, losing 27–0, becoming the fourth side ever to be held scoreless in a Challenge Cup Final at Wembley, after Halifax, Widnes and Barrow were kept scoreless in three consecutive finals between 1949 and 1951. During the last 10 minutes of the Challenge Cup Final, St. Helens received a penalty that was well within kicking range for either of their goal kickers, Great Britain international [[Paul Loughlin]], and Australian dual international [[Michael O'Connor (rugby)|Michael O'Connor]]. No wanting to be another team to be held scoreless in a Cup Final at Wembley, Murphy frantically gestured from the sideline to team captain [[Paul Vautin]] to take the kick at goal despite the scoreline. Vautin ignored Murphy's instructions and instead the ball was kicked into touch for a tap restart. Wigan's defense held firm though and St. Helens last scoring opportunity was lost. This led to tension between Murphy and Vautin for a number of years until they worked together for the Australian [[Nine Network]] during television coverage of The Ashes series during the [[1994 Kangaroo tour]].{{cite web|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iQOV5yIETtw |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/varchive/youtube/20211214/iQOV5yIETtw |archive-date=2021-12-14 |url-status=live|title=1989 Challenge Cup Final highlights|via=YouTube|date=31 December 2017|access-date=1 January 2018}}{{cbignore}} In 1991, he joined [[Huddersfield Giants|Huddersfield]]: within a year, promotion to the Second Division had been achieved. After a six-year absence from rugby league, Murphy returned to the game in 2003 as football director of Leigh after the [[RFL Championship|National League One]] club sacked coach [[Paul Terzis]].{{cite news | title = Murphy returns to Leigh | work = BBC Sport | place =UK | publisher = [[BBC]] | date = 4 September 2003 | url = http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/3082250.stm | access-date =24 April 2010}} ==Post-retirement== In the [[1999 New Year Honours]], Murphy was awarded the OBE for services to the game of rugby league.{{London Gazette |issue=55354 |date=31 December 1998 |page=12 |supp=1}} In 2000 his autobiography, ''Saint and Sinner'' was published.{{cite book | last = Murphy | first = Alex | title = Saint and Sinner: The Autobiography of a Rugby League Legend | publisher = Mainstream | year = 2000 | location = UK | isbn = 9781840183092 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=NYQNAAAACAAJ | id = 1840183098, 9781840183092 }} Murphy was also employed as a commentator by BBC television for a number of years working alongside long-time league commentators [[Eddie Waring]], and later former dual rugby international [[Ray French]]. He also worked for Australia's [[Nine Network]] as an expert commentator during the [[1994 Kangaroo tour]]. He was also employed to write opinion columns for newspapers such as the [[Daily Mirror]], and the [[Manchester Evening News]]. One was known as 'Murphy's Mouth.' In 2006 he became Chairman of [[Oxford Cavaliers]] rugby league club. Murphy is an inductee in both the St Helens RFC Hall of Fame,{{cite web|url=http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=13|title=St Helens Hall of Fame|publisher=saints.org.uk|date=31 December 2011|access-date=1 January 2012|archive-date=27 May 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120527234636/http://www.saints.org.uk/saints.org.uk/home/viewpage.php?page_id=13|url-status=dead}} and the Warrington Wolves RLFC Hall of Fame.{{cite web|url=http://www.wire2wolves.com/halloffame.php?player_id=87&browse_level=2|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130410082511/http://www.wire2wolves.com/halloffame.php?player_id=87&browse_level=2|url-status=dead|archive-date=10
